name: mtp-m16.5-gdn-chain-shuffle-reductions
hardware: AMD Radeon Pro W7900 (gfx1100, 48 CUs, wavefront=32)
change: Replace the 4 per-token LDS+__syncthreads block tree-reductions (q_sum, k_sum, kv_mem, out) with warp-shuffle reductions (block_all_reduce_sum), and launch the block as a single wavefront (32 threads) when head_k_dim<=128 so the reductions are pure __shfl_xor with zero __syncthreads and zero reduction LDS. Falls back to 64 threads (2-warp shuffle+LDS combine) for head_k_dim in {192,256}. Only the chain kernel is changed; the tree DFlash kernel is untouched.

correctness.microbench_oracle: scripts/gdn_chain_microbench.py vs numpy f32 delta-rule reference: out_max_abs 9.5e-7, leaf(state)_max_abs 6.0e-8 (essentially bit-exact; KL gate 0.05 trivially met)
correctness.tests: 30 GDN/linear_attn pytest cases pass; tree kernel unchanged

measurement.harness: scripts/mtp_verifier_rocprof.py --backend hip_gfx1100 --chain-attn-mode batched --decode-tokens 8 --candidate-budget 3 (6 verify passes, gate off)

notes: GDN chain recurrence is the biggest single verify-cycle kernel family. The GPU is already saturated (4096 blocks on 48 CUs) so the win is per-block: removing the LDS tree-reduction syncs. ~8.3% on the kernel; verify-cycle C_B impact is incremental (-0.20 ms/pass of a ~13.6 ms/pass kernel budget). Next GDN lever: the per-(v_head,t) scalars (q_scale, k_scale, beta, decay incl. exp/softplus transcendentals) are recomputed by all 128 dv blocks of each head -- a tiny pre-pass could remove that redundancy.
